quiz_questions.question_text: Sound is what type of wave?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave consisting of alternating compressions and rarefactions of the medium. It requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate.

quiz_questions.question_text: The speed of sound in air at room temperature is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound travels at approximately 343 m/s in air at 20°C. This speed increases with temperature and is much faster in liquids and solids than in gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which medium does sound travel fastest?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound travels fastest in solids (e.g., ~5100 m/s in steel), intermediate in liquids (~1480 m/s in water), and slowest in gases (~343 m/s in air). Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines the pitch of a sound?
quiz_questions.explanation: Pitch is the perceptual quality of sound determined by its frequency. Higher frequency corresponds to higher pitch, and lower frequency to lower pitch.

quiz_questions.question_text: The human ear can typically detect sound frequencies in the range of:
quiz_questions.explanation: The audible range for humans is approximately 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz). Frequencies below 20 Hz are called infrasound, and above 20 kHz are ultrasound.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sound wave has a frequency of 440 Hz in air (v = 340 m/s). What is its wavelength?
quiz_questions.explanation: λ = v/f = 340/440 ≈ 0.773 m ≈ 0.77 m. The note A4 (440 Hz) has a wavelength of about 77 cm in air.

quiz_questions.question_text: What property of sound is most closely related to its loudness?
quiz_questions.explanation: The loudness of a sound is primarily determined by its amplitude (the maximum pressure variation). Greater amplitude means more energy and louder sound.

quiz_questions.question_text: An open pipe of length L has its fundamental resonant frequency at:
quiz_questions.explanation: For an open pipe (open at both ends), the fundamental has antinodes at both ends and one node in the middle: λ = 2L, so f = v/(2L).

quiz_questions.question_text: When two tuning forks of slightly different frequencies are sounded together, what is heard?

options.text: A sound at double the original frequency
Complete silence due to destructive interference

quiz_questions.explanation: When two tones of slightly different frequencies interfere, they produce beats — periodic fluctuations in loudness. The beat frequency equals the absolute difference of the two frequencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: A closed pipe (closed at one end, open at the other) of length 0.5 m produces its fundamental frequency. If the speed of sound is 340 m/s, what is the fundamental frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: For a closed pipe, the fundamental wavelength is λ = 4L = 4 × 0.5 = 2.0 m. So f = v/λ = 340/2.0 = 170 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'intensity' of a sound wave?

options.text: The power delivered per unit area perpendicular to the wave direction
The speed of the sound wave
The frequency of the sound wave

quiz_questions.explanation: Sound intensity is the power (energy per second) transmitted through a unit area perpendicular to the wave's direction. It is measured in watts per square meter (W/m²).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'decibel' (dB) mean in acoustics?

options.text: A logarithmic unit used to measure the intensity level of sound

quiz_questions.explanation: The decibel (dB) is a logarithmic scale for expressing sound intensity level: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing. Each 10 dB increase represents a tenfold increase in intensity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'beat frequency'?

options.text: The average frequency of two interfering waves
The fundamental frequency of a vibrating string
The absolute difference between the frequencies of two interfering sound waves

quiz_questions.explanation: The beat frequency is |f₁ − f₂|, the absolute value of the difference between two simultaneously sounding frequencies. It represents the rate at which the loudness fluctuates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'timbre' refer to in sound?

options.text: How loud or soft a sound is
The quality or character of a sound that distinguishes different instruments playing the same note
How high or low the pitch is

quiz_questions.explanation: Timbre (tone color) is what makes a piano and a violin sound different even when playing the same note at the same volume. It depends on the mixture of harmonics present in the sound.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'ultrasound'?

options.text: Sound that can only travel through solids
Sound below the threshold of human hearing intensity
Sound that travels at supersonic speeds
Sound with frequency above 20,000 Hz, beyond the upper limit of human hearing

quiz_questions.explanation: Ultrasound refers to sound waves with frequencies above 20 kHz, beyond the range of human hearing. It is widely used in medical imaging, sonar, and industrial testing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'harmonic' in the context of sound and vibration?

options.text: A beat produced by two different instruments
Any random frequency produced by a vibrating object
The lowest possible frequency of a system
A frequency that is an integer multiple of the fundamental frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: A harmonic is any frequency that is a whole-number multiple of the fundamental frequency. The 1st harmonic is the fundamental, the 2nd harmonic is twice the fundamental, and so on.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resonance' mean for a sound-producing system?

options.text: The amplification of sound when a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the system
The change in pitch due to motion
The gradual absorption of sound energy
The reflection of sound off a hard surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Resonance in a sound system occurs when an external vibration matches one of the system's natural frequencies, causing a dramatic increase in amplitude and loudness.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'infrasound'?

options.text: Very quiet sound near the threshold of hearing
Sound of very high pitch beyond human hearing
Sound with frequency below 20 Hz, beneath the lower limit of human hearing
Sound that travels at subsonic speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: Infrasound consists of sound waves below 20 Hz, which are too low-pitched for human ears to detect. Elephants and some natural phenomena (earthquakes, volcanoes) produce infrasound.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'fundamental frequency' mean?

options.text: The frequency at which sound becomes inaudible
The highest frequency a system can produce
The average of all harmonic frequencies
The lowest natural frequency at which a system vibrates

quiz_questions.explanation: The fundamental frequency is the lowest resonant frequency of a vibrating system. For a string or pipe, it corresponds to the simplest standing wave pattern with the fewest nodes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'Mach number'?

options.text: The frequency of a sonic boom
The decibel level of the loudest possible sound
The number of harmonics an instrument can produce
The ratio of an object's speed to the speed of sound in the surrounding medium

quiz_questions.explanation: Mach number is the ratio of an object's speed to the local speed of sound: M = v_object/v_sound. Mach 1 is the speed of sound, Mach 2 is twice the speed of sound, and so on.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pipe organ in a church has an open pipe that is 3.4 m long. What is the fundamental frequency of this pipe if the speed of sound in the church is 340 m/s?
quiz_questions.explanation: For an open pipe, f₁ = v/(2L) = 340/(2 × 3.4) = 340/6.8 = 50 Hz. This low fundamental frequency produces a deep bass tone.

quiz_questions.question_text: A guitar tuner sounds a 440 Hz tuning fork alongside a guitar string. The player hears 3 beats per second. If the guitar string's frequency is known to be slightly sharp, what is the string's frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: Beat frequency = |f₁ − f₂| = 3 Hz. Since the string is sharp (higher than the tuning fork), f_string = 440 + 3 = 443 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: A doctor uses an ultrasound machine at 5 MHz to image a fetus. If the speed of sound in tissue is approximately 1540 m/s, what is the wavelength of the ultrasound in the tissue?
quiz_questions.explanation: λ = v/f = 1540 / (5 × 10⁶) = 3.08 × 10⁻⁴ m = 0.308 mm. This short wavelength allows high-resolution imaging of small structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: A train horn emits a frequency of 500 Hz. As the train approaches a station at 25 m/s, a waiting passenger hears the horn. After the train passes and moves away, the passenger notices the pitch drop. What are the observed frequencies when approaching and receding? (v_sound = 340 m/s)
A student blows across the top of a test tube that is 17 cm long and closed at the bottom. What is the fundamental frequency of the sound produced? (v_sound = 340 m/s)
quiz_questions.explanation: A test tube acts as a closed pipe. f₁ = v/(4L) = 340/(4 × 0.17) = 340/0.68 = 500 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rock band performs at 110 dB near the stage. If the threshold of hearing corresponds to 0 dB (I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m²), what is the intensity of the sound at 110 dB?
quiz_questions.explanation: β = 10 log(I/I₀), so 110 = 10 log(I/10⁻¹²). Therefore log(I/10⁻¹²) = 11, giving I = 10¹¹ × 10⁻¹² = 10⁻¹ W/m² = 0.1 W/m².

quiz_questions.question_text: A ship uses sonar to detect the ocean floor. A sound pulse is emitted and its echo returns in 0.8 seconds. If the speed of sound in seawater is 1500 m/s, how deep is the ocean at that point?
quiz_questions.explanation: Total round-trip distance = v × t = 1500 × 0.8 = 1200 m. Since the pulse travels down and back, depth = 1200/2 = 600 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two identical fire alarms are placed in a room. One alarm alone produces a sound level of 80 dB at a certain point. If both alarms sound simultaneously from the same location, what is the approximate sound level at that point?
quiz_questions.explanation: Doubling the intensity adds about 3 dB: 10 log(2I/I₀) = 10 log(I/I₀) + 10 log(2) ≈ 80 + 3 = 83 dB. Decibels are logarithmic, so doubling intensity does not double the dB level.

quiz_questions.question_text: A flute player in an orchestra plays a note at the third harmonic of an open pipe 0.6 m long. What frequency does the audience hear? (v_sound = 340 m/s)
quiz_questions.explanation: For an open pipe, f_n = nv/(2L). The third harmonic: f₃ = 3 × 340/(2 × 0.6) = 1020/1.2 ≈ 850 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a thunderstorm, a person sees lightning and hears thunder 6 seconds later. If the speed of sound is 340 m/s, approximately how far away did the lightning strike?
quiz_questions.explanation: Distance = speed × time = 340 × 6 = 2040 m ≈ 2 km. Light arrives nearly instantaneously, so the delay is essentially the travel time of sound.
